cards.text: An {{c1::unnamed namespace}} (also called an {{c2::anonymous namespace}}) is a namespace without a name: {{c3::namespace { }}}

cards.front: What linkage do identifiers in an unnamed namespace have?
cards.back: Internal linkage. All identifiers in an unnamed namespace are treated as if they have internal linkage, limiting them to the current translation unit.

cards.question: How do you access members of an unnamed namespace?
cards.answer:
value = 10;
doSomething();

No prefix needed - they're accessible directly in the same file.
cards.explanation: Unnamed namespace members can be accessed without any prefix within the same translation unit. They act like global identifiers with internal linkage.

cards.front: What is the modern C++ way to give internal linkage to functions and variables?
cards.back: Use unnamed namespaces instead of the 'static' keyword. This is the preferred modern C++ approach.

cards.question: Modernize this code using an unnamed namespace:
cards.explanation: Unnamed namespaces are the modern C++ way to create identifiers with internal linkage. Prefer them over 'static' for functions and variables.

cards.text: An {{c1::inline namespace}} is declared with the {{c2::inline}} keyword: {{c3::inline namespace v1 { }}}. Its members are treated as if they're part of the parent namespace.

cards.front: What is the primary use case for inline namespaces?
cards.back: Versioning - to maintain multiple versions of an API while making one version the default. Libraries can update the default version without breaking existing code.

cards.question: How can you access both versions in this code?
cards.answer:
mylib::doSomething(); // v2 (default via inline)
mylib::v1::doSomething(); // v1 (explicit)
mylib::v2::doSomething(); // v2 (explicit)
cards.explanation: The inline namespace makes its members accessible without the namespace name. Non-inline versions require explicit qualification.

cards.back: Yes, but it's not common. Unnamed namespaces can be nested inside named namespaces or other unnamed namespaces.

cards.front: What's the difference between 'static' and unnamed namespaces for internal linkage?
cards.back: They're functionally similar, but unnamed namespaces are the modern C++ approach. Unnamed namespaces work for all identifiers (including types), while 'static' doesn't work for types.

cards.concept: When to use unnamed vs inline namespaces
cards.explanation:
Use unnamed namespaces for:
  • Implementation details that should be file-local
  • Helper functions/variables used only in one file
  • Modern alternative to 'static' for internal linkage
Use inline namespaces for:
  • Versioning APIs in libraries
  • Providing a default version while maintaining backward compatibility

cards.text: All identifiers in an {{c1::unnamed namespace}} have {{c2::internal linkage}}, limiting them to the current translation unit.
An {{c1::inline namespace}} makes its members accessible as if they're part of the {{c2::parent namespace}}, commonly used for {{c3::versioning}}.
The modern C++ way to give internal linkage is to use {{c1::unnamed namespaces}} instead of the {{c2::static}} keyword.
Inline namespaces are primarily used for {{c1::API versioning}}, making one version the {{c2::default}} while maintaining backward compatibility.
